APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(5): This is a clearly relevant dataset-and-evaluation contribution for NLP/ML: a new publicly released corpus of annotated revisions (three drafts per essay, sentence alignments, two-tier purpose labels) directly supports argument mining and discourse research. The paper targets the right audience by demonstrating both corpus analyses and replication/extension of Zhang & Litman (2015) with SVM classifiers, using expected evaluation standards (unweighted average F-score, kappa, participant cross-validation). While the corpus focuses on argumentative college essays (native and L2), that scope is appropriate for top-tier venues interested in discourse, educational NLP, and revision analysis. | B(4): The paper addresses a clearly NLP-relevant topic (argumentative revision analysis / argument mining) and releases a new annotated corpus with sentence-level alignments and fine-grained revision purposes that would be useful to the community. The experimental setup (replicating Zhang & Litman 2015, SVM classifiers, 10-fold participant cross-validation, unweighted F-score, and inter-annotator kappa) follows standards expected at top venues. It targets the right audience and tasks for an NLP/ML conference, but has minor scope concerns: the corpus is limited to college argumentative essays (native and L2) and the work lacks reported code/hyperparameter details and direct measures of writing-quality improvement, which slightly reduce broad applicability.

reviewer_a.justification: This is a clearly relevant dataset-and-evaluation contribution for NLP/ML: a new publicly released corpus of annotated revisions (three drafts per essay, sentence alignments, two-tier purpose labels) directly supports argument mining and discourse research. The paper targets the right audience by demonstrating both corpus analyses and replication/extension of Zhang & Litman (2015) with SVM classifiers, using expected evaluation standards (unweighted average F-score, kappa, participant cross-validation). While the corpus focuses on argumentative college essays (native and L2), that scope is appropriate for top-tier venues interested in discourse, educational NLP, and revision analysis.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper addresses a clearly NLP-relevant topic (argumentative revision analysis / argument mining) and releases a new annotated corpus with sentence-level alignments and fine-grained revision purposes that would be useful to the community. The experimental setup (replicating Zhang & Litman 2015, SVM classifiers, 10-fold participant cross-validation, unweighted F-score, and inter-annotator kappa) follows standards expected at top venues. It targets the right audience and tasks for an NLP/ML conference, but has minor scope concerns: the corpus is limited to college argumentative essays (native and L2) and the work lacks reported code/hyperparameter details and direct measures of writing-quality improvement, which slightly reduce broad applicability.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper addresses an important gap by releasing a new publicly available corpus of annotated revisions for argumentative essays with three drafts, sentence-level alignments, and a two-tier (Surface vs. Content) schema with eight fine-grained categories, including data from both native and L2 college students. The corpus enables reproducible work (they replicate and extend Zhang & Litman 2015, showing SVMs with argument-mining/discourse features improve over a unigram baseline) and supports both behavioral analysis of student revisions and automatic revision-purpose classification. While limitations (no direct measures of writing-quality improvement, sentence-level granularity, and domain focus on college argumentative essays) constrain generalizability, the resource is likely to open meaningful research directions and be built upon by NLP and educational technology researchers. | B(4): The paper provides a new publicly released, richly annotated corpus of revisions (three drafts per essay, sentence-level alignments, two-tier schema with eight fine-grained categories) including both native and L2 college students, which fills a clear resource gap for argument-mining and writing-revision research. The authors demonstrate concrete applications (statistical analysis of revision behavior and training/evaluation of revision-purpose classifiers with improved performance over a unigram baseline), indicating immediate utility for building and evaluating tools. While the domain focus (college argumentative essays) and lack of direct measures of writing-quality improvement limit generality, the dataset and annotation scheme are likely to be widely used and to open significant new research directions in automated feedback and revision analysis.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses an important gap by releasing a new publicly available corpus of annotated revisions for argumentative essays with three drafts, sentence-level alignments, and a two-tier (Surface vs. Content) schema with eight fine-grained categories, including data from both native and L2 college students. The corpus enables reproducible work (they replicate and extend Zhang & Litman 2015, showing SVMs with argument-mining/discourse features improve over a unigram baseline) and supports both behavioral analysis of student revisions and automatic revision-purpose classification. While limitations (no direct measures of writing-quality improvement, sentence-level granularity, and domain focus on college argumentative essays) constrain generalizability, the resource is likely to open meaningful research directions and be built upon by NLP and educational technology researchers.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper provides a new publicly released, richly annotated corpus of revisions (three drafts per essay, sentence-level alignments, two-tier schema with eight fine-grained categories) including both native and L2 college students, which fills a clear resource gap for argument-mining and writing-revision research. The authors demonstrate concrete applications (statistical analysis of revision behavior and training/evaluation of revision-purpose classifiers with improved performance over a unigram baseline), indicating immediate utility for building and evaluating tools. While the domain focus (college argumentative essays) and lack of direct measures of writing-quality improvement limit generality, the dataset and annotation scheme are likely to be widely used and to open significant new research directions in automated feedback and revision analysis.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper provides a useful new corpus and demonstrates two concrete applications (statistical analysis of revisions and SVM-based revision-purpose classification) with 10-fold participant cross-validation and improvement over a unigram baseline, which supports the main claims. However, no ablation or sensitivity analyses are reported, hyperparameters and code are not mentioned, and reproduction details are incomplete despite using known alignment and SVM methods. Related work coverage is described as adequate and the authors discuss several limitations (domain, sentence-level granularity, lack of direct writing-quality evaluation), but the experimental evaluation lacks some important analyses that would strengthen the claims. | B(3): The experiments are adequate to support the main claims: a new annotated corpus is released and classifiers (SVM with discourse features) improve over a unigram baseline using 10-fold participant cross-validation and reporting F-scores, and the paper replicates prior work. However, no ablation or sensitivity analyses are reported, hyperparameters are not provided, and code availability is not mentioned, which limits reproducibility. Related work is covered adequately and several limitations (domain scope, sentence-level granularity, lack of direct writing-quality measures) are explicitly discussed, but important experimental details and deeper analyses are missing.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper provides a useful new corpus and demonstrates two concrete applications (statistical analysis of revisions and SVM-based revision-purpose classification) with 10-fold participant cross-validation and improvement over a unigram baseline, which supports the main claims. However, no ablation or sensitivity analyses are reported, hyperparameters and code are not mentioned, and reproduction details are incomplete despite using known alignment and SVM methods. Related work coverage is described as adequate and the authors discuss several limitations (domain, sentence-level granularity, lack of direct writing-quality evaluation), but the experimental evaluation lacks some important analyses that would strengthen the claims.

reviewer_b.justification: The experiments are adequate to support the main claims: a new annotated corpus is released and classifiers (SVM with discourse features) improve over a unigram baseline using 10-fold participant cross-validation and reporting F-scores, and the paper replicates prior work. However, no ablation or sensitivity analyses are reported, hyperparameters are not provided, and code availability is not mentioned, which limits reproducibility. Related work is covered adequately and several limitations (domain scope, sentence-level granularity, lack of direct writing-quality measures) are explicitly discussed, but important experimental details and deeper analyses are missing.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper’s main novelty is the release of a new annotated corpus of argumentative revision with three drafts per essay, sentence-level alignments, and a two-tier (Surface vs. Content) schema with eight fine-grained categories — including essays from both native and L2 college students — which is a clear, tangible resource contribution beyond prior high-school work. The authors also clearly differentiate their contributions and demonstrate applications (statistical analysis and replication/extension of Zhang & Litman 2015). However, the modeling methods are largely a replication/extension of existing SVM-based approaches rather than novel algorithms, so the contribution is notable primarily for the resource and task setup rather than new techniques. | B(3): The main novelty is a new publicly released corpus of annotated argumentative-revision data (three drafts per essay, sentence-level alignments, and a two-tier revision schema with eight categories), which is a meaningful dataset contribution and extends prior corpora to college and L2 writers. However, the methods largely reuse existing alignment and classification approaches (Zhang & Litman) and the modeling is a replication/extension rather than a new technique. The paper clearly differentiates its contributions from prior work and broadens the resource/application space, so the contribution is moderately novel but essentially incremental.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper’s main novelty is the release of a new annotated corpus of argumentative revision with three drafts per essay, sentence-level alignments, and a two-tier (Surface vs. Content) schema with eight fine-grained categories — including essays from both native and L2 college students — which is a clear, tangible resource contribution beyond prior high-school work. The authors also clearly differentiate their contributions and demonstrate applications (statistical analysis and replication/extension of Zhang & Litman 2015). However, the modeling methods are largely a replication/extension of existing SVM-based approaches rather than novel algorithms, so the contribution is notable primarily for the resource and task setup rather than new techniques.

reviewer_b.justification: The main novelty is a new publicly released corpus of annotated argumentative-revision data (three drafts per essay, sentence-level alignments, and a two-tier revision schema with eight categories), which is a meaningful dataset contribution and extends prior corpora to college and L2 writers. However, the methods largely reuse existing alignment and classification approaches (Zhang & Litman) and the modeling is a replication/extension rather than a new technique. The paper clearly differentiates its contributions from prior work and broadens the resource/application space, so the contribution is moderately novel but essentially incremental.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper’s central claims (release of a three-draft, sentence-aligned annotated corpus and demonstration of two use cases) are clearly stated and supported by the described data collection, alignment correction, annotation schema, and classification experiments using SVMs with argument-mining/discourse features vs. a unigram baseline evaluated with 10-fold participant cross-validation and unweighted average F-score. There are no mathematical derivations to check, and the experimental setup uses reasonable baselines and evaluation metrics. Minor technical omissions include lack of reported hyperparameters and no explicit mention of statistical significance testing details for the reported "significant" improvements, but these do not substantially undermine the presented evidence. | B(4): The paper’s main claims — release of a three-draft, sentence-aligned, purpose-annotated argumentative revision corpus and two applications (statistical analysis and SVM-based revision-purpose classification) — are supported by the described evidence (manual-corrected alignments, inter-annotator Kappa, 10-fold participant cross-validation, unweighted average F-score) and successful replication/extension of Zhang & Litman (2015). There are no mathematical derivations to assess. Methodologically it is sound in using a reasonable baseline and evaluation metric, but reproducibility/detail concerns remain (no hyperparameters or code reported, no mention of statistical significance testing). Overall, these are minor issues that do not undermine the main technical conclusions.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper’s central claims (release of a three-draft, sentence-aligned annotated corpus and demonstration of two use cases) are clearly stated and supported by the described data collection, alignment correction, annotation schema, and classification experiments using SVMs with argument-mining/discourse features vs. a unigram baseline evaluated with 10-fold participant cross-validation and unweighted average F-score. There are no mathematical derivations to check, and the experimental setup uses reasonable baselines and evaluation metrics. Minor technical omissions include lack of reported hyperparameters and no explicit mention of statistical significance testing details for the reported "significant" improvements, but these do not substantially undermine the presented evidence.

Overall, these are minor issues that do not undermine the main technical conclusions.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well organized and follows a clear flow (introduction → method → experiments → conclusion), and its main contributions—the new corpus and two-tiered revision schema—are stated early and presented clearly. Notation and evaluation metrics are consistently defined (e.g., unweighted average F-score, kappa) and the two-tier schema with eight categories gives a comprehensible framework for annotations. Writing quality is reported as good and related work coverage adequate, though minor presentation omissions (e.g., no mention of hyperparameter or code availability) slightly reduce completeness of the experimental description. | B(4): Based on the provided summary, the paper is well organized (clear intro, method, experiments, conclusion) and the main contributions are stated early and repeatedly. Notation and methodological descriptions appear consistent and understandable, and the writing quality is reported as good. The summary does not detail figures or tables, so while the overall presentation is clear, there may be minor presentation gaps (e.g., unspecified visual aids) preventing an exceptional rating.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is well organized and follows a clear flow (introduction → method → experiments → conclusion), and its main contributions—the new corpus and two-tiered revision schema—are stated early and presented clearly. Notation and evaluation metrics are consistently defined (e.g., unweighted average F-score, kappa) and the two-tier schema with eight categories gives a comprehensible framework for annotations. Writing quality is reported as good and related work coverage adequate, though minor presentation omissions (e.g., no mention of hyperparameter or code availability) slightly reduce completeness of the experimental description.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper replicates and extends the Zhang and Litman (2015) method (SVM with argument-mining/discourse features) and evaluates it against a unigram baseline on the same newly released corpus using the same metric (unweighted average F-score) with 10-fold participant cross-validation, which is a fair comparison. It reports significant improvements over the unigram baseline and situates results relative to prior high-school writing findings, and related work coverage is described as adequate. However, only a unigram baseline and the replicated prior method are reported (no additional modern baselines or ablations and hyperparameters/code are not mentioned), so comparisons are good but not fully comprehensive. | B(4): The paper replicates and extends the relevant prior method (Zhang & Litman 2015) and compares it to a unigram baseline, using the same corpus, 10-fold participant cross-validation and unweighted average F-score, so comparisons are fair. Related work coverage is described as adequate and the replication demonstrates similar improvements to prior high-school results. However, stronger or more varied baselines beyond a unigram model are not reported, and the summary does not indicate a detailed analysis of when prior methods might perform comparably or better. Overall the comparisons are good but have minor omissions in breadth and depth of analysis.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper replicates and extends the Zhang and Litman (2015) method (SVM with argument-mining/discourse features) and evaluates it against a unigram baseline on the same newly released corpus using the same metric (unweighted average F-score) with 10-fold participant cross-validation, which is a fair comparison. It reports significant improvements over the unigram baseline and situates results relative to prior high-school writing findings, and related work coverage is described as adequate. However, only a unigram baseline and the replicated prior method are reported (no additional modern baselines or ablations and hyperparameters/code are not mentioned), so comparisons are good but not fully comprehensive.

main_claims: A new publicly released corpus of annotated revisions for argumentative essays is presented, comprising three drafts per essay with sentence-level alignments and purpose labels.

The corpus uses a two-tiered revision schema (Surface vs. Content-based) with eight fine-grained categories related to argument mining/discourse analysis, and includes annotations for essays from both native and L2 college students.

The paper demonstrates two applications of the corpus: (1) statistical analysis of student revision behaviors and (2) training/evaluation of automatic revision-purpose classification, replicating and extending Zhang and Litman (2015).

The method from Zhang and Litman (2015) (SVM with argument-mining/discourse features) yields significant improvement over a unigram baseline for several classification tasks on this corpus, similar to prior results on high-school writing.
_summary.methodology: Collected three drafts of argumentative essays by college students, automatically aligned sentences (using Zhang and Litman 2014 algorithm) and manually corrected alignments, then annotated revision purposes with a two-tier schema (Surface vs Content-based with eight subcategories); used SVM classifiers with unigram baseline and additional Location/Textual/Language features evaluated with 10-fold participant cross-validation reporting unweighted average F-score.
limitations: No evaluative data available to directly measure improvement in writing quality; degree of improvement approximated by number of revisions.
Development of a full revision analysis system is outside the scope of this work.
Corpus currently focuses on argumentative essays by college students (native and L2), limiting domain/generalizability.
Annotation granularity is sentence-level and current revision categories could be further refined (authors propose more fine-grained categories and additional properties as future work).
_summary.topic_area: NLP - argumentative revision analysis / argument mining
